signals: Base rebuilding is clearly underway: long-term load (fitness proxy) climbed steadily from late January into early March, reaching series highs.

Training stimulus has been volatile overall (large single-day spikes + multiple zero-load days), but the most recent ~2 weeks show much higher training frequency (fewer zeros) and a tighter cluster of mid/high daily loads.
The highest stress concentration occurred in the week ending 2026-03-08: it combined the highest 7-day load with the highest monotony and strain in the dataset.
Modeled freshness (TSB) has been strongly negative during spike weeks, but it rebounds quickly with even a single zero-load day (high responsiveness of acute load).
Running VO2max estimates stepped down from 54 → ~51 in mid-February, briefly touched 50, and have been stable at 51 in the most recent updates; cycling VO2max slipped 60 → 58 by 2026-03-01.
Early-series uncoupled ACWR/log metrics are distorted by an artificially tiny shifted chronic baseline (startup artifact) and should not be interpreted as true spikes.
evidence: Chronic EWMA (28d EWMA fitness proxy) rose ~3.5× from the local minimum 21.9 (2026-01-29) to the peak 77.6 (2026-03-08). Acute EWMA peaked at 111.3 (2026-03-08) after bottoming at 10.9 (2026-01-29).
7-day rolling load (acute_7d_sum) ranged from 28.1 (2026-01-23/24) to a series high 798.0 (2026-03-08). It fell to 624.8 on 2026-03-09 after a zero-load day.
Garmin-like baseline (chronic_28d_avg of 7d sums) increased from 224.7 (2026-01-30) → 357.0 (2026-02-28) → 471.8 (2026-03-08) → 483.0 (2026-03-09, series high).
Stress concentration peaked late: monotony_7d hit 2.34 and strain_7d hit 1866.8 on 2026-03-08 (both series highs). The only other strain >1500 was 1525.6 on 2026-03-07 (monotony 2.13).
Modeled freshness (TSB = chronic_ewma - acute_ewma) reached very negative values on major spike days: -47.8 (2026-02-15), -33.7 (2026-03-08). After rest, TSB improved to -11.3 (2026-03-09).
Ramp rate (ramp_7d) spiked during the biggest builds: 28.1 (2026-02-15, series max) and 23.9 (2026-03-08).

Uncoupled EWMA spike indicator: acwr_uncoupled peaked at 3.64 (2026-02-15; log_ratio 1.29), and was 2.07 on 2026-03-08 (log_ratio 0.73). Note: values on 2026-01-12/13 are extreme because shifted chronic (chronic_uncoupled) was ~0.8 before a 7-day baseline existed.

Training Status snapshot (VO2 dated 2026-03-08): acute_load 629, chronic_load 503, ACWR 1.2. This broadly aligns in magnitude with the load-history series near 2026-03-09 (acute_7d_sum 624.8; chronic_28d_avg 483.0; coupled ACWR 1.29), suggesting small method/weighting differences and/or the snapshot being effectively ‘current’ while VO2 is last updated 2026-03-08.
implications: Quantitatively, the athlete now has a meaningfully higher load baseline than in late January (chronic metrics at or near series highs), consistent with “base rebuilding.”

The dominant risk flag in the data is not just ‘high load’ but ‘high load + high monotony’: the week ending 2026-03-08 paired peak weekly load with peak monotony/strain, meaning stress was accumulated with limited day-to-day variation.
Because acute load (and TSB) responds quickly to low-load days (e.g., 2026-03-08 → 2026-03-09), small reductions can materially change readiness signals without collapsing the chronic baseline.

VO2max estimates are currently best described as stable/plateaued (running ~51) over the last ~1–2 weeks of updates; there isn’t clear evidence of a rising VO2max estimate yet alongside the recent load build (not implying causation—just describing the concurrent trends).
uncertainty: Load units are unspecified and training-status load/ACWR likely use Garmin’s proprietary weighting; interpret absolute values cautiously and prioritize within-series trends.
14 of 57 days have daily_load = 0.0; these could be true rest days or missing capture (device not worn / activity not counted), which changes how we interpret training frequency and monotony.

Monotony_7d and strain_7d show 0.0 on 2026-01-23 to 2026-01-25 despite non-zero acute_7d_sum (and a non-zero daily_load on 2026-01-25), suggesting an edge-case or artifact in those calculations for that period.
VO2max series are sparse/stepwise by nature; the long-term running VO2max value of 66.0 on 2025-11-17 is a clear outlier relative to adjacent points (52.0 on 2025-10-17 and 54.0 on 2026-01-12).

signals: The athlete is in a fast rebuild phase: chronic load has climbed rapidly since late January, but the ramp has been achieved via a few pronounced spike blocks rather than a smooth progression.

The data shows a repeating pattern of: (1) load spike week(s) with very negative TSB and high ACWR, followed by (2) partial unloading that brings ACWR <1 and TSB positive/near-zero—i.e., the system responds well to deliberate downshifts.
The most recent build (late Feb → early Mar) tightened training frequency (far fewer zero-load days), which increased monotony and strain substantially.

VO2max estimates (running, cycling) have drifted slightly downward compared with early-season values and are now stable at their new level; this provides a “performance estimate baseline” heading into spring races.
evidence: Garmin-like baseline (chronic_28d_avg) rose from ~224.7 (2026-01-30) to 483.0 (2026-03-09), indicating the athlete is currently carrying the highest 28-day baseline in the provided window.

Spike block examples:
- 2026-02-15: daily_load 242.0 (max), acute_7d_sum 672.1, coupled ACWR 2.82, uncoupled 7d/28d ACWR 2.63, TSB -47.8, ramp_7d 28.1.
- 2026-03-08: acute_7d_sum 798.0 (max), monotony 2.34 (max), strain 1866.8 (max), uncoupled 7d/28d ACWR 2.19, TSB -33.7, ramp_7d 23.9.

Recovery/downshift examples:
- 2026-02-22 to 2026-02-23: acute_7d_sum 219.4, coupled ACWR ~0.70–0.71, TSB +4.6 to +12.0.
- 2026-01-21 to 2026-01-24: sustained low loads pushed TSB positive (+7.4 to +14.2) with very low coupled ACWR (0.13–0.84).
Recent frequency shift: from 2026-02-24 to 2026-03-09 there is only 1 zero-load day (2026-03-09). Earlier in the dataset there are 14 zero-load days total (14/57).

VO2max (running): 54.0 early (through 2026-02-06), then 51.0 starting 2026-02-13, with brief 50.0 on 2026-02-27 and 2026-03-02, returning to 51.0 by 2026-03-04 through 2026-03-08. Cycling VO2max: 60.0 (2026-02-22/25) then 58.0 (2026-03-01).
implications: Macrocycle opportunity: you now have a materially higher chronic baseline (chronic_28d_avg ~480–500) to build from toward the A-priority 8-hour adventure race on 2026-05-30 (≈82 days from 2026-03-09). The data suggests consolidation (fewer spikes) could be the best way to extend this baseline sustainably.

Load architecture guardrails suggested by your own spike history (heuristics to calibrate):
- Ramp_7d: try to keep most builds in the single digits to low teens; the two biggest ramp peaks (28.1 on 2026-02-15; 23.9 on 2026-03-08) coincided with the most negative TSB and highest spike ratios.
- Uncoupled 7d/28d ACWR: treat >2.0 as “spike territory” (seen 2026-02-15 and 2026-03-07/08). Staying ~1.0–1.6 appears more controlled.
- Monotony: weeks with monotony >2.0 (2026-03-07/08) produced extreme strain (>1500). Planning more variability (not necessarily less total load) is a key lever to reduce strain.

Sustainable ceiling insight: the dataset proves you *can* reach acute_7d_sum ~800, but that came with the highest strain and deeply negative TSB; for 12–24 week planning, it may be better to treat ~800 as an occasional peak rather than a repeating target until you have a longer history confirming tolerance.

VO2max trend stability (running ~51) can be used as a monitoring baseline; if load continues to climb without upward movement in VO2 estimates over many weeks, that’s a useful ‘signal to re-check the load mix’—but the current data is too sparse to draw strong conclusions.
uncertainty: This load history is only 57 days long; identifying a true long-term sustainable chronic load ceiling (for a full season) requires more months of stable training data.
Because chronic_28d_avg is still being pulled upward by the recent high weeks, it may temporarily over-represent what’s sustainable once race-specific phases or tapers occur.
VO2max estimates are device-derived and reported sparsely; the dataset cannot distinguish true trend from estimation noise, especially with multi-week gaps earlier in the year.

Calculation artifacts exist (monotony/strain = 0 on 2026-01-23 to 2026-01-25; extreme uncoupled ACWR before shifted chronic baseline exists), so avoid over-weighting those edge periods in planning rules.

signals: The week ending 2026-03-08 was the single most concentrated stress block in the dataset (highest 7-day sum, highest monotony, highest strain).
A single zero-load day on 2026-03-09 materially reduced acute load and improved modeled freshness, indicating readiness metrics are currently very sensitive to small load reductions.
Chronic baseline is at/near its maximum (chronic_28d_avg 483.0 on 2026-03-09), so the primary near-term need is controlling spikes/monotony rather than pushing baseline up as fast as possible.
Current spike indicators have improved from the peak but remain elevated: coupled ACWR 1.29 and uncoupled 7d/28d ACWR 1.65 on 2026-03-09.
evidence: Current day (2026-03-09): daily_load 0.0; acute_7d_sum 624.8; chronic_28d_avg 483.0; coupled ACWR 1.29. Acute_ewma 83.5; chronic_ewma 72.2; TSB -11.3. Monotony 1.60; strain 998.6.

Peak stress day (2026-03-08): acute_7d_sum 798.0 (max); monotony 2.34 (max); strain 1866.8 (max); acute_ewma 111.3 (max); chronic_ewma 77.6 (max); TSB -33.7; ramp_7d 23.9. Uncoupled 7d/28d ACWR 2.19; acwr_uncoupled 2.07 (log_ratio 0.73).
Strain threshold evidence: strain_7d exceeded 1500 only on 2026-03-07 (1525.6) and 2026-03-08 (1866.8).

Upcoming race inside the next 28 days: Flirt with Dirt 10k trail race on 2026-03-28 (19 days after 2026-03-09). The next race (Dirty Dash trail half) is 2026-04-18, which is 40 days out (outside the 28-day window).
implications:
Immediate acute-load guardrails (based on your own ‘peak stress’ signatures; not workout prescriptions):
- Treat acute_7d_sum ~800 as a practical “do not repeat casually” ceiling (it coincided with peak monotony/strain and TSB -33.7 on 2026-03-08).
- A near-term operational cap of ~650–700 for acute_7d_sum can keep you below the recent red-flag zone while maintaining a high chronic baseline (2026-03-09 is already 624.8).

ACWR guardrails:
- Prefer uncoupled 7d/28d ACWR ~1.0–1.6 as a controlled build/consolidation band.
- Treat >2.0 (uncoupled 7d/28d) as spike territory (seen on 2026-03-07/08 and 2026-02-15). 2026-03-09 is 1.65—close enough that another high week could push back above 2.0 quickly.

TSB (modeled freshness) guardrails:
- The deepest negative TSB values in this dataset cluster around spike weeks (e.g., -47.8 on 2026-02-15; -33.7 on 2026-03-08). A useful near-term rule is avoiding multiple consecutive days below ~-25.
- Current TSB -11.3 suggests moderate accumulated fatigue: you’re not in a “fully fresh” state, but you’re also not at the recent extreme.

Monotony/strain management:
- Keep monotony <2.0 most weeks; once monotony crossed 2.0 (2026-03-07/08), strain jumped into the highest range (>1500).
- Use strain as a combination alarm: aim to keep most weeks under ~1200–1500; >1500 occurred only at the top end of your current block.

Race proximity (2026-03-28) within this 28-day window:
- Given the current high baseline and recent spike signals, the highest-value metric objective is preventing another spike week (ACWR >2 or strain >1500) that could disrupt consistency heading into late March/early April.
- Because readiness signals improved sharply with one low day (2026-03-09), small load reductions can be an effective lever to manage race-week freshness without giving up the chronic base.
uncertainty: Training Status acute/chronic load and ACWR are undated in the snapshot; they align best with ~2026-03-09 magnitudes, but exact correspondence to the load-history rows is not guaranteed.
Zero-load days may include missing data (non-wear or untracked activities), which would understate acute load and overstate recovery.
VO2max updates may not occur frequently within the next 28 days; absence of VO2 changes during this window would not necessarily imply absence of fitness change.
ACWR/TSB/monotony/strain thresholds are heuristic; this short dataset can highlight *relative* risk moments (your spikes), but cannot yet define your personal ‘safe maximums’ with high confidence.
